body: More honest ones, too. Those graphs that are plastered all over every single piece of climate change literature I've ever seen are misleading at best. Yes, it's true that humans are most likely altering the Earth's climate and that the consequences could be catastrophic, but 150 years of instrument temperature records are not remotely close to being enough to show a meaningful trend. Statements like "2016 is the hottest year on record" are similarly misleading. Yes, it's the hottest year in the 150 years worth of temperature data we have, but it's such a minuscule data set that the fact that it's the hottest out of it is pretty much irrelevant to everything. Most people haven't been educated enough to really understand climate change, but most are smart enough to tell when they're being manipulated. If we want to end the denialism, we ought to start with education and leave out the manipulation and fear mongering.
label: Climate denial critique

body: > For more than 50 years, members of the local community have advocated at all levels of government for conservation of this range and access to the lands for recreation and cultural uses. Now, a diverse group of supporters—including local elected officials, Indigenous leaders, veterans, area business owners, faith leaders, and community groups—are urging President Joe Biden to designate this approximately 7,000-acre area as the Castner Range National Monument. Aside from securing the benefits mentioned above, national monument designation would advance environmental justice and promote equitable access to nature. It would also help address climate change—protected natural areas sequester and store more carbon than developed areas do—and allow the land to continue to collect and filter precipitation, which local communities rely on for drinking water. > > Safeguarding Castner Range as a national monument would also facilitate important cultural and ecological landscape connections for the world's largest binational, or cross-border, community—more than 2 million people, with more than 25 million yearly crossings between El Paso and the neighboring Mexican city, Ciudad Juarez. > > And the designation would also safeguard cultural sites and practices dating back 10,000 years, increase the representation of Latino heritage sites in U.S. protected areas, and honor the land's military legacy. > > During the last century, Castner Range was used for military training. Although that has waned, some of the range's lands are still part of Fort Bliss and remain closed to the public. Other parts of Castner Range have been included in various proposed development plans that would threaten the range's natural, cultural, and economic value.

body: Science, in its pure form, is an unbiased approach to solving problems by proposing a solution and testing through experimentation. To many people, however, science seems to stand for anti-religious, anti-conservative ideology. The biggest reason people have for opposing climate change is this: they see science as inherently opposed to their interests and take anything that comes as a result of it as counter their interests. There is also some nuance to the debate: mainly, what is the human impact? This is essentially the key feature of the debate. On one side, you must consider that the earth has a cyclical process - there is a natural cycle of change. On the other hand, humans are making emitting more than ever, and it is a indisputable fact that the greenhouse effect happens. Regardless of where you stand on it, most of the disagreement is over the degree of human involvement, and people take the extremes of this debate (1: it's a conspiracy: there is no human involvement, and 2: it's all our fault) as the debate itself
label: Climate information

body: How much electricity does the traditional monetary system use? This isn't a rhetorical question, nor is this an attempt to redirect attention via whataboutism. The reason I ask is because I feel like it'd be more useful to compare bitcoin to the traditional monetary system (centralized data centers, financial institutions, precious metal mining, wire transfer systems, material for paper currency and coins, printing, minting, transportation, etc). I just want to make sure that we are accurately assessing the situation, and I don't see how we can do that without considering the energy usage of what bitcoin was meant to replace. Don't get me wrong though! Regardless of the answer to my question, I'm still all for supporting the cryptocurrencies known to be ultra energy efficient: Cardano, Iota, Solana, Nano, Polkadot, Tezos… really, every cryptocurrency that avoids relying on proof-of-work consensus mechanisms. It's an understatement to say that climate change is a serious problem, so we need to look for every opportunity to slow it down and reverse it. So even if bitcoin is actually more energy efficient than the traditional monetary system, it still shouldn't ultimately be the winner. I'm rooting for non-PoW cryptocurrencies because they are _way_ more eco-friendly than anything else. Edit: Also, I don't mean to imply that this infographic is meaningless. I hope my post didn't come across that way! Getting a sense of how much energy bitcoin uses is valuable information, and comparing it to nations does that effectively. I just think it'd be good to also show how much the energy the traditional monetary system uses since bitcoin was never intended to replace Norway.
label: Climate action critique

body: Vote in every election. Vote with your wallet, choosing sustainable and locally produced goods when you can. (Look for local farmers' markets and CSAs.) Switch to a plant-based diet. Grow a veggie and pollinator garden. (Look for a community garden.) Compost your food waste. (It has a bigger impact than recycling.) Plant trees. (See if your local park dept has a tree planting program.) Spend less time looking at screens (it costs a lot of energy to keep servers running). Buy less of everything — shop second-hand, and repair things instead of replacing them. Walk, bike, carpool, or use public transport whenever you can. Do an energy audit and [calculate your carbon footprint]([URL] Make changes accordingly. Finally, one I don't see on these kinds of impact lists often, but which belongs here: Be a witness to this moment on earth. Join a birding or wildlife club, hike or spend time outdoors. Whatever interests you. Even if we cannot stop or reverse climate change as much as we want to, we can witness what we're losing.
label: climate activism

body: This seems like a classic case of "I want X but I don't actually want to deal with the consequences of X". There aren't really many options for you to contrive this scenario. The orbit could be highly elliptical, but this would still ultimately mean a 24-day year which is already pretty absurd. That puts your planet closer to its star than mercury is to ours. Without magic, your only real options are some sort of huge, regular occurrence that could trigger a season-like change in temperature. Like a migration of birds so massive that they blot out the sun for a long period of time. As for how this huge axial tilt doesn't already influence the climate, your only real option is something similar that conveniently counteracts the would-be shift in climate during the year in a similar manner. But it would be almost impossible to avoid making this feel very forced.

body: >Well first of all I don't think most antinatalists are misanthropic, Okay well I do. I've made many comments explaining why so I won't rehash it here. Feel free to look around. >If you ask some prospective parents why they want a child usually it involves them maybe wanting to live vicariously through another person, having someone to care for them when they're older, trying to fix a broken marriage, to seek meaning in their lives, providing another sibling for an already existing child, to continue the family name, or just because they're cute. I don't think most parents have children for those reasons, but more importantly I don't think those reasons propel them through raising the child to adulthood. To be candid, this reminds me of the antinatalist-lite people I know in my personal life who express their resentment towards friends having children by framing their divergent choices as somehow more responsible or self-sacrificing. Caitlyn says she decided not to have kids because she doesn't want to contribute to climate change. Her inability to keep a boyfriend has nothing to do with it. I find it transparent and totally unconvincing. >If you have the child it will be harmed and it will have these good things but I don't see those good things as a justification for bringing it into existence. Extend that reasoning to yourself - nothing you will have or experience is worth continuing so you can't justify your own existence. Why are you still here? The answer is obvious: you don't believe that with regard to yourself. So for some reason we ought to assume that every child who comes into being will not find their way to where you are: a place where existence is better kept than discarded. You and others offer these nonsensical arguments that amount to treating existence like a party that you didn't want to go to and are now too anxious to leave. You text all your friends telling them not to come and you could leave whenever you want, but you just stick around munching on snacks and reaching into this grab bag of inane excuses for why you can't leave. But the truth is you actually enjoy the party well enough to stay. That's what I mean when I talk about narcissism: the belief that your experience is in some way exceptional and worth preserving while others should never begin. >Humans have a fundamental drive to continue so it seems callous to me to place someone in a position where you know their main interest will be thwarted and there's nothing they can do about it. When I read things like this...it just brings [Veruca Salt]([URL] to mind. To get so many years of life only to whine about them eventually ending strikes me as a profound ingratitude; it takes some gall to suggest that the people who gave you the ability to feel anything at all are callous because they knew the ride would end. Again: this is a correctable circumstance. >here I'd like to give you an analogy. It was a bad analogy. >I'd say something similar about bringing someone into existence, if they respond well to adversity and hardship that's all to the better but the fact that they respond well doesn't give you a rationale for bringing them into existence in the first place. Well that presumes my primary concern is this feeble suffering-avoidance regime you all seem to favor. I don't. I think it's obviously ridiculous. >You keep speaking about a life that's worth living and how you enjoy it and that's fine since it's your life but why would we bring these guaranteed to suffer people into existence in order to bestow some benefit that no one needs? In fact, it appears that everyone alive who doesn't plan to kill themselves in the immediate future has concluded that life is better kept than discarded. That tells me my kids will probably have that feeling one day. body: China, Germany urge US not to ditch Paris climate accord Date 22.05.2017 At climate change talks in Berlin, Germany and China have called for the US to remain a party to the Paris climate accord. US President Donald Trump has said the US might pull out, having called global warming a hoax. Both Germany and China on Monday urged the US to stand by its signing of the Paris Accord, ahead of an expected decision on whether Washington will ditch the agreement. In a joint statement at the beginning of the Petersburger Climate Dialogue in Berlin, the two countries' environment ministers urged Washington not to abandon the agreement. "We are making representations at every level for the USA to remain part of the climate change agreement," said German Environment Minister Barbara Hendricks. Hendricks said she believed the US remaining inside the agreement would shore up efforts to curb rising temperatures, and that it could also be economically advantageous for the US. Read more: Optimism at climate conference, despite US hesitation US President Donald Trump has already signaled a wish to withdraw from the Paris Accord.The president once said climate change was a hoax invented by China to weaken the US in terms of exports. Hendricks stressed that, while any US abandonment of the treaty would be damaging, it need not be the end. "In the case of an exit, it would be crucial for most of the rest of the world to remain in favor of climate protection," said Hendricks. "I am convinced that we will succeed. There will be no exit domino effect." China, which was also one of the parties to the treaty, has said it will implement the agreement "100 percent." Chinese Environment Minister Xie Zhenhua echoed the words of his president, Xi Jinping, alluding to the responsibility for future generations. "This is the position of China with regard to the current uncertainties," Xie said. Representatives from some 30 countries around the world were present in Berlin on Monday for the talks which will take place over two days. The second day of the conference will see German Chancellor Angela Merkel take to the podium for a keynote speech. The Prime Minister of Fiji, Josaia Voreqe - whose nation will chair a UN climate conference to be held in the German city of Bonn in November - is also set to address delegates. The Paris Accord seeks to keep the rise in temperatures within two degrees Celsius (3.6 Fahrenheit) above pre-industrial levels, with a more optimistic limit of 1.5 degrees Celsius. body: I think the subjectivity matters. Mathematical theory is pure logic; there isn't any space to debate unless you disagree with the definition of a term. Because all terms in math are rigorously defined, this doesn't happen. But, if you follow the science metaphor, by moving up to Physics, we have increasing degrees of subjectivity. You have bodies that interact by unknown mathematical functions, and you are trying to apply evidence to reverse-engineer the problem. Physical arguments are logical based on evidence, but, there is always the chance that you haven't observed the whole possible set of data, and your answer is an approximation instead of the full truth. This is what happens in sociological settings. You can observe A, B, C sets of evidence, typically, certain factors correlating with certain outcomes (women typically have lower income than men in the same positions, for example) but you have no specific data that gives you the cause for the observed trends. You can make an argument on historical evidence (as we do) and you can compare how much evidence you have towards your argument with how much stands against it, but, this is difficult: you would have to know a VAST amount of data, and humans are biased against looking to refute their own arguments. A single individual generally can't do this, but a whole field of academia could. That's why peer review and competition in the academy is so important to validating theory. Now, consider a science: Climate Change. If you look into the research, you'll find papers are 99:1 in favor of human-influenced climate change. The academy has tried to refute the hypothesis of human-influenced changed, and come out overwhelmingly for it. BUT, if you listen to televised debates, you get 1:1 perspective. That's an improper critique of the hypothesis. It is true, of course, that conversations are generally between two people. To convince someone they don't hold a substantiated opinion, you don't want a whole room of people screaming at them. But this is what happens in feminist debate; those who don't understand or disagree with the hypothesis (like women making less than men because they are women) attempt to refute this with a single feminist rather than reading up on the magnitude of evidence that supports a claim. And they think if they cannot be convinced by this one person, then they are right. That's pitting anecdotal evidence against actual science, and it's harmful to discourse. This is why a "deep knowledge" of feminism is insisted on; otherwise we aren't debating you, we're trying to educate you and you're acting like you can pick and choose facts. I think QUESTIONING is fine as long as you understand you are here to learn: if you don't have the background, you can't talk to a theory at the same level as those who do. Unfortunately, feminism and social science have more real-world implications to most laymen than physical laws (gravity will do its thing whether you understand it or not).

body: With most degrees you can do something to positively impact climate change if you wanted. You could be an electrical eng and deal with power grid upgrades, a physicist and deal with climate modelling, a mech eng and work with designing low emission vehicles, work in finance funding ethical industries or even political science to drive policy action. However, I'd say chem eng is the best all round degree for understanding climate issues at the core. Not many people understand how interconnected and difficult this issue is to solve - we can't just convert everything to solar and wind. What about cement and steel production? What about energy security? What about transportation? It's incredibly difficult to get something (clean energy) for nothing. It's easy to say stop oil and gas production, but as a cheme you truly get a sense for the scale and complexity around why this isn't so simple. The field is all about assessing trade-offs and optimising systems, which is why I believe this degree is so powerful in addressing climate change if you choose to go down that path.

body: First, let's be clear about terminology: [[URL] "Glaciologically, ice age is often used to mean a period of ice sheets in the northern and southern hemispheres; by this definition we are still in an ice age (because the Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets still exist). More colloquially, when speaking of the last few million years, ice age is used to refer to colder periods with extensive ice sheets over the North American and Eurasian continents: in this sense, the most recent ice age ended about 10,000 years ago." During the Quaternary glaciation, there have been cold periods (glacials) and warm periods (interglacials) with a cycle length of \~100,000 years related to Milankovitch cycles. [[URL] The Younger Dryas was not a full glacial period, and it was not an ice age. It was a period of rapid regional (particularly North Atlantic), not global, cooling following the Late Glacial Interstadial. In your own link it says temperatures in Antarctica rose during the Younger Dryas. A proper glacial period involves long global cooling and glaciation. > Do you disagree that the precursors of the Younger Dryas, as explained here, are occurring at this present time? There is evidence for a slowdown of thermohaline circulation in the northern Atlantic. Broecker, W. S. (1999). What if the conveyor were to shut down? Reflections on a possible outcome of the great global experiment. GSA Today, 9(1), 1-7. "Past shutdowns of the Atlantic Ocean's conveyor circulation appear to have played a key role in triggering the large and abrupt global climate changes that punctuated the last period of glaciation including the millennial duration Younger Dryas (Broecker and Denton, 1990). Modeling studies suggest that the ongoing greenhouse warming and consequent strengthening of the hydrologic cycle might trigger yet another such shutdown (Manabe and Stouffer, 1993; Stocker and Schmittner, 1997)... But is it realistic to believe that a shutdown of the conveyor a century or so from now would produce the conditions that characterized the last glacial period? The answer is very likely "no," for several reasons. The first has to do with the fact that during the Younger Dryas, Canada and Scandinavia still had sizable ice caps. The second is that the abrupt part of the warming at the close of the Younger Dryas brought climate only about halfway to its interglacial state (Severinghaus et al., 1998). The other half of the transition was more gradual, reflecting perhaps the post–Younger Dryas retreat of the residual ice caps in Canada and Scandinavia. Finally, modeling studies (Manabe and Stouffer, 1993; Stocker and Schmittner, 1997) that forecast a greenhouse-induced conveyor shutdown do so only after a substantial global warming (4 to 5 °C) has occurred. Hence, the global climate conditions prevailing at the time of the shutdown would be substantially warmer than those that existed just before the onset of the Younger Dryas. For these reasons, the analogy to the conditions that prevailed during the Younger Dryas surely constitutes a worst case scenario." Shakun, J. D., & Carlson, A. E. (2010). A global perspective on Last Glacial Maximum to Holocene climate change. Quaternary Science Reviews, 29(15-16), 1801-1816. "The magnitude of the Younger Dryas climate anomaly (cooler/drier) increases with latitude in the Northern Hemisphere, with an opposite pattern (warmer/ wetter) in the Southern Hemisphere reflecting a general bipolar seesaw climate response. Global mean temperature decreased by \~0.6 C during the Younger Dryas. Therefore, our analysis supports the paradigm that while the Younger Dryas was a period of global climate change, it was not a major global cooling event but rather a manifestation of the bipolar seesaw driven by a reduction in Atlantic meridional overturning circulation strength." [[URL] "The most recent assessment of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in its Special Report on the Oceans, Cryosphere and Climate Change (SROCC) concluded the AMOC is very likely to weaken over the 21st Century. A slowdown would have a cooling effect around the North Atlantic region. However we expect that this effect would be smaller than the warming from greenhouse gases, leading to a reduced warming around the North Atlantic. Even more dramatic changes are theoretically possible as there may be a 'tipping point' beyond which the current strong AMOC becomes unsustainable. Furthermore, it's possible that if the AMOC were to shutdown it may not be able to recover...A shutdown in the 21st Century is considered very unlikely (though plausible). The likelihood increases beyond 2100, at least for higher emissions scenarios (see IPCC SROCCC report). Climate model projections of the future of the AMOC range widely, and therefore climate scientists see understanding more about the potential future risks to this important system as a key priority." So yes, I agree with you that weakening of the thermohaline circulation in the northern Atlantic Ocean is a concern. It is important to note this: [[URL] "A shutdown of the AMOC has large climate impacts, with pronounced cooling over the North Atlantic and smaller amounts of warming in the SH21. However, the impacts of passing an AMOC tipping point would be huge, so it is best viewed as a "low probability, high impact" scenario."

body: There's lots of room up here, and plenty of fresh water too. OTOH, look at places like Arizona that have seen a huge influx of population in recent decades, despite their being naturally inhospitable to human beings well before there was much warming of the climate. As I say, humans are very adaptable and getting more so all the time. That's most clear not only in intensely hot places like Arizona, but in intensely cold ones like where I live. Last year, we went from Christmas Day through New Year's without the temperature ever rising above zero Fahrenheit: the highest "high" temperature was -2. But our well-insulated homes stayed cozy and warm, and our power and internet and smartphones continued to work, and our showers continued to provide us with hot water. Despite all the handwringing from some quarters, in the past 25 or 30 years, the planet has seen an incredible reduction in hunger and extreme poverty. We'll use our economic and technological power to manage this too. It may be that a technological fix actually reverses the warming. Or we'll just adapt to it. But I guarantee you, this world in 50 or 100 years isn't going to be some blighted wasteland with humans gone or staggering about like it's "The Walking Dead". At least not due to climate change. As long as we avoid falling victim to a paper-clip maximizing AI or the like, people will be healthier and wealthier. If the climate is dramatically different from what it is now, we'll get used to it, and young people won't have known anything different. It will look quaint that so many people from the early 21st century were in such a tizzy over it all.

body: There's a strain of environmentalism that kind of drives me crazy, the "corporations are all at fault and if we held them accountable we could stop climate change without asking anyone to make any sacrifices" when, in reality, holding polluters accountable would then cause everyone to radically change consumption habits. Animal agriculture is a huge polluter, to hold Big Ag accountable would involve taxing meat production which would then require everyone to eat less meat. Holding oil companies accountable is a great idea! But such a thing would translate to shutting down oil wells, raising gas taxes, road tolling, and carbon taxes, all of which would translate to everyone driving less. In addition to this, we need to stop urban sprawl building our cities more dense as apartment buildings are far and away more energy efficient, allowing more people to take public transit, bike, or walk to work. This isn't to say alternatives shouldn't be subsidized and the government shouldn't make the change less harsh (subsidies to make meat alternatives cheaper and to increase public transit will absolutely be apart of this equation) but people who think we can stop climate change without changing anyone's consumption habits are either woefully delusional or are lying.

Come easter you were told by your parents that in the night a magic bunny came and laid colored eggs for you to find. And sure enough you went outside and there were the eggs. body: >Positive feedback loops tend to hit hard ceilings pretty fast. For example, cancer cells die when the host die, they do not take over the world like so much grey goo. A self-improving AI is a positive feedback loop; its ceiling might be hardware-related. Well cancer is something that can only exist in a particular host environment. If the environment allows, a feedback loop can continue forever. Earth's biological history has involved successive waves of bacteria, plants, etc exploding to cover large sections of the planetary surface within very short evolutionary time. A self-improving intelligence wouldn't face any barriers to existence as it improved; better and better algorithms will make a given quantity of hardware more productive. You're right that there's probably some kind of soft limit where you approach nearly optimal algorithms and then the problems for improvement get so difficult that they can no longer be quickly solved with a given amount of hardware. But the issue here is that we have a very poor grasp on what "some kind of soft limit" looks like. All of our data points on intelligence come from the breadth of human capacities, some weak AI that we've developed, and a bunch of unintelligent animals. We don't understand the concept of general intelligence well enough to say what its limits are, as we can with mathematical problems where we often know the minimum running time of any algorithm that could solve them. >Genetic algorithms perform horribly badly whenever the problem space is not carefully constrained. Note how this is at odds with the idea of AGI. Few people, if any, see genetic algorithms as the main path to AGI. >Assume that some AI has managed to obtain enough of the world's computational resources to turn itself into a human-level AI. This means that the AI was previously strictly dumber than a human, and so these vast computational resources were already available to some human actor (possibly the AI's operators). This is already pretty much a doomsday scenario; adding an AGI into the mix won't change much. New algorithms can scale onto new hardware. Also, AIs are already better than humans at narrow range of intellectual tasks. The idea of AGI is a misnomer because by the time it is human-level in every major domain, it will very likely be much better than us in some of them. So the worrisome scenarios are likely to be weird cases where our relative intellectual capacities are asymmetric. >Any time discounting of utils below, say, 2% per year just sounds completely silly to me. There are other doomsday scenarios that are more pressing, for example climate change. I see any discounting above 0% as silly but to each their own.

body: This article implicitly frames "the CO2 problem" in terms of the mean modeled ECS. Food for thought: In 5 out of 6 rounds of Russian Roulette, the player survives. Does the average person typically decide whether or not to play a round of Russian Roulette based on the mean expected outcome? Failure to take emergency action on the climate crisis is an obscene game of Russian Roulette not only with our own lives but with the lives of our children and the lives and safety of billions of people around the world. If you are not blocking roads to demand emergency action on the climate crisis at this point in the game you are pissing in the wind. The problem is too many people trust their naive political intuition more than the empirical evidence. The empirical evidence is crystal clear: 30 years have passed since the 1st IPCC report told us all we need to know to solve the climate crisis. No representative democracy has come anywhere close to taking the necessary emergency action to end fossil fuels through more than 3 decades of elections. 30 years of voting, lobbying, letter writing, donating, emailing and peaceful marches have summed up to one point in history: polite, respectable ABJECT FAILURE on the climate and ecological crisis. On the other hand, the historical and sociological evidence is very strong that nonviolent civil resistance wins radical political change, very rapidly, in dozens of case histories, in societies across the world, for the last hundred years or so. In fact, no civil resistance movement in the last century failed once it had mobilized just 3.5% of the population into active support of its goals. The lives and livlihoods of thousands of millions of people are on the line. The continued existence of the US as a relatively prosperous, free and democratic country is on the line. The climate crisis is the greatest crime, the greatest moral obscenity, in human history. We are in a crisis that demands emergency action! Blocking roads in great numbers is how people around the world force radical political change all the time over the last hundred years. In Puerto Rico they just forced the governor to resign by blocking roads. In Chile they are re-writing their Constitution because the people forced a referendum by blocking roads. In Serbia they brought down the violent dictator Slobodan Milosevic by blocking roads, same with Ferdinand Marcos in the Phillipines. Blocking roads in mass numbers does indeed force the positive political change, over and over, across the world. It's the same principle as a labor strike- ordinary people acting in mass have the power to force government to a halt until their demands are met. The strategy is sound. It requires mass participation to win. So, what excuse do you have for not getting in the road to demand emergency climate action? Go to [URL] to sign up! body: >In the past we expected it to be their job. You bring up the example of global climate change. I am not a politician nor am I lobbied and I promise you I know more about this issue, hell even economically, than the average politician. No one pays me to be a smart person who knows things and yet I am. It doesn't sound like you really understand how busy our politicians are. They spend vast amounts of time actually working on legislation, spending time in committees, speaking with constituents, etc. They also have a large number of LAs on staff who's job it is to do all the fact finding and then summarize it for their boss so then when they go a meeting or whatever they are informed. But part of that fact finding process involves talking to people who are very familiar with an issue and those people are generally lobbyists. Indeed, lobbyists often build excellent working relationships with LAs because the information they provide is invaluable to the LAs job. I also want to make it clear, as a lobbyist the majority of your time will be spent with LAs. Oh by the way, I am not sure how familiar you are with Congressional staffers but an LA is a Legislative Assistant and avoiding gross consolidation even if it leaves a couple large competitors (telecom). The laws are not cosmetic. Once again, under Bush these laws were not enforced as vigorously as they had been in the past, that is beginning to change though it might be until the next president that we see a return to the old status quo. >We would need less tax revenue if we didn't spend in the billions to not only give tax cuts but also give subsidy to these very industries. First we don't spend anything giving companies tax cuts, second subsidies make up a very small portion of our annual budget. For instance in 2005 they spent $57 billion on subsidies to businesses of all kinds (though the largest portion goes to farmers) and in 2006 it was $95 billion.
label: climate policy critique
